title: Scaling taste, craft, and quality in the AI era
speakers: Teo Connor (VP Design, Airbnb), Loredana Crisan (Chief Design Officer, Figma)
key_points: Care in design is reframed as 'hosting' (an Airbnb value): removing friction, adding delightful moments, and getting consistent details right so users feel considered, Connor's test is whether someone walks in and 'feels hosted.' Loredana adds that every design is 'like throwing a great party.'

The most valuable emerging skills as AI generates abundant output are judgment, editing, and evaluation, the ability to look at lots of work (your own, your team's, and the AI's) and say 'Not that, this.' Airbnb cultivates this 'old school' by having people share work and process to build a collective understanding of what good looks like.

Solo-with-an-agent work is a 'seductive narrative' but inferior; the real magic comes from diverse, T-shaped people (deep craft plus breadth) plus AI mixed together. Airbnb uses small cross-functional 'pod' teams run like startups, with high learning agility, high agency, and entrepreneurial spirit ('embrace the adventure').

Advice for early-career designers: when you start with a new tool you do your best work via brute force, then 'get good' and start shortcutting to 95%; resist the tools when they say first output is good enough and push for the extra 5%, that 5% is what makes ideas stand out.

Connor predicts an interim period of 'an outsized amount of very bad design' and 'slop,' but is bullish on AI for the industry because she believes in human creativity; what must survive is human dreaming and great design, judgment, editing, and the ability to simplify.
